coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based room-temperature phosphorescent test strip: Visual and convenient water detection in organic solvents
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bing
Yuan, Xiaoying
Lv, Xu
Mei, Yongjun
Peng, Huaqiao
Li, Lin
Guo, Yong
Du, Juan
Zheng, Baozhan
Xiao, Dan
coredata.dc:description: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) has got more and more attentions in sensing due to its specific optical properties of longer lifetime and higher signal-to-noise ratio. However, developing low-cost RTP test strip (RTP-TS) for visual and convenient detection is still a challenging. Here, we reported the construction of new RTP-TS based on N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), which was synthesized with a simple hydrothermal method using isophthalic acid (IPA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors. The constructed RTP-TS exhibited bright green RTP emission under UV excitation (365 nm) with a longer lifetime of 0.769 s. The mechanism studying indicates the efficient RTP emission of RTP-TS is from the N/O-rich CDs and the anchor effect of polyacrylic acid (PAA), which can restrict the varication/rotation of N-CDs by H-bonds, and suppress the nonirradiative quenching processes. Nevertheless, the presence of H2O can destroy these H-bonds and therefore effectively quench the RTP emission of RTP-TS, the changes of RTP intensity is inversely proportional to H2O concentration. Based on this, the RTP-TS has been successfully used for visual and convenient water detection in several common organic solvents (ethanol, acetone, acetonitrile, tetrahydrofuran) with high sensitivity, excellent stability, accuracy and reliability. This work will open a new approach to construct visual RTP-TS for practical use in sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room-temperature phosphorescence
Test strip
Carbon dots
Visual water detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
Preparation of N-CDs based RTP test strip (RTP-TS)
RTP-TS detection of water in organic solvents
RTP mechanism for N-CDs and N-CDs/PAA
Detection of water in organic solvents

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Sichuan Province Science and Technology Support Program

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The diagram for the synthesis of N-CDs and N-CDs/PAA. Phosphorescent images of solid N-CDs (I) and N-CDs/PAA (II) after turning off the UV light irradiation (365 nm). (b) Construction of RTP-TS and its phosphorescent response to water at room temperature.

(a) The TEM image of synthesized N-CDs. Inset: The particle size distribution of N-CDs. (b) The FT-IR spectra of N-CDs and IPA. (c) The XPS survey scan of N-CDs. (d–f) The high-resolution XPS of C 1s (d), N 1s (e), and O 1s (f), respectively.

(a) The UV–Vis absorption (black), fluorescence excitation (red) and emission (blue) spectra of N-CDs aqueous solution, respectively. Insets: the photographs of N-CDs aqueous solution under the irradiation of daylight (I) and UV light (II), respectively. (b) The phosphorescent emission (green) and excitation (red) spectra of N-CDs powder. Insets: fluorescence (I) and phosphorescence (II) images of solid N-CDs under UV light irradiation (365 nm). (c) The phosphorescence (green) and fluorescence (blue) emission and excitation (black) spectra of N-CDs/PAA powder. Inset: fluorescence (I) and phosphorescence (II) images of solid N-CDs/PAA under UV light (365 nm). (d) Time-resolved photoluminescence decay spectra and fitting curves of solid N-CDs/PAA and N-CDs powders recorded at 510 nm under the excitation of 350 nm. (e) The images of N-CDs/PAA powder under UV lamp (365 nm) on and off with time.
(a) The energy-level diagram of the relevant photophysical processes for N-CDs, and its response to water. (b) Schematic of RTP quenching in N-CDs/PAA films by water.

(a, d) Corresponding FL and PL photographs of RTP-TS in ethanol and acetone solvent with different water contained (0–20% for ethanol, 0–8% for acetone, v/v) under a UV lamp (365 nm) ON and OFF. (b, e) Phosphorescence emission spectra of RTP-TS in anhydrous ethanol (b) and acetone (e) with different water contained (1.0–30.0% for ethanol, 0.2–13.0% for acetone, v/v). (c, f) Plots of the quenching efficiency (P/P0) of RTP-TS versus different water content according to (b) and (e), respectively.

(a) The photostability of RTP-TS in ethanol, acetone, ACN and THF under continuous UV irradiation (365 nm, 6 W), respectively. (b) The response of 10 pieces of RTP-TS to water in ethanol solvent without (red) and with (black) water present.
Determination of water in real organic solvents samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The average of the three times test results.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The difference between two water contents in the same solvent.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based room-temperature phosphorescent test strip: Visual and convenient water detection in organic solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, No. 29 Wangjiang Road, Chengdu, 610064, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, No. 29 Wangjiang Road, Chengdu 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Research Institute of Civil Aviation Administration of China (CAAC)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Second Research Institute of Civil Aviation Administration of China (CAAC), Chengdu 610041, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, No. 29 Wangjiang Road, Chengdu, 610064, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) has got more and more attentions in sensing due to its specific optical properties of longer lifetime and higher signal-to-noise ratio. However, developing low-cost RTP test strip (RTP-TS) for visual and convenient detection is still a challenging. Here, we reported the construction of new RTP-TS based on N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), which was synthesized with a simple hydrothermal method using isophthalic acid (IPA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors. The constructed RTP-TS exhibited bright green RTP emission under UV excitation (365 nm) with a longer lifetime of 0.769 s. The mechanism studying indicates the efficient RTP emission of RTP-TS is from the N/O-rich CDs and the anchor effect of polyacrylic acid (PAA), which can restrict the varication/rotation of N-CDs by H-bonds, and suppress the nonirradiative quenching processes. Nevertheless, the presence of H2O can destroy these H-bonds and therefore effectively quench the RTP emission of RTP-TS, the changes of RTP intensity is inversely proportional to H2O concentration. Based on this, the RTP-TS has been successfully used for visual and convenient water detection in several common organic solvents (ethanol, acetone, acetonitrile, tetrahydrofuran) with high sensitivity, excellent stability, accuracy and reliability. This work will open a new approach to construct visual RTP-TS for practical use in sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is the source of life for all things, and plays vital role in human being survival and production. However, the presence of water in organic solvents is generally identified as the most common impurity [1]. For example, trace water in organic solvents may determine the reaction reactivity, lower the yields, or decrease the reaction products [2]. In addition, it is very difficult for experimenters to realize water detection due to the ubiquity of water in samples. As a traditional method, Karl Fischer titration has been used widely and considered as the most reliable method for trace water monitoring in solvents [3]. However, the disadvantages of lacking continuous monitoring, requiring specific instruments, using toxic chemicals, complex and long-time operating seriously limit its further applications. Therefore, developing simple and sensitive analytical methods for accurate quantification of water in organic solvents is of high important and common analytical problems in laboratory and industrial applications.

Recently, various methods of electrochemistry [4], photometry and luminescence [2,5,6] have been developed and widely used for water detection in organic solvents, and the fluorescent-based optical strategies have been considered as a powerful and reliable detection method due to its advantages of high sensitivity, lower detection limits, shorter response time, real-time monitoring, and so on [7–9]. Compared to fluorescence, room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) has recently become a very useful mode of detection for optical sensing applications due to its longer lifetime of emission, larger Stokes’ shifts and the superior selectivity [10,11]. The longer lifetime of RTP can avoid the interferences from any short-lived fluorescence emission and scattering light [12], which can lead to the greatly enhanced sensitivity and selectivity of sensors [13–15]. Therefore, RTP sensor has greater advantages than fluorometry, and RTP materials played an extremely important role in sensor construction [16–18]. Based on this, various materials with excellent phosphorescent properties have been developed for RTP sensor, such as organic compounds [19], semiconductor quantum dots [20], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [21], and so on. However, these RTP materials usually include the disadvantages of complex and time-consuming synthesis procedures, high cost and toxicity, lower photo-stability [22,23], which is not favour of practical applications. Therefore, it is very significant and urgent to design simple, eco-friendly, and reliable RTP materials for optical sensors.

As a new fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have become an active research area due to its excellent luminescence performance, good biocompatibility, ease of synthesis and tuneable composition [24–26]. Recently, there has been an extended effort to develop CDs as a fluorescent material for detection in organic solvents [27–29]. However, these sensors are based primarily on the fluorescence of CDs rather than their phosphorescence. In fact, CDs can also be utilized as an ideal RTP material due to its unique nature of special optical properties and easy-to-be functionalization [30,31]. However, synthesis of phosphorescent CDs is still a challenging because phosphorescence can be easily hampered by the spin suppression property of triplet excitons transitions, the non-radiation decay process or oxygen-quenching under ambient conditions [32,33]. To address this problem, heteroatoms (such as N, O) were introduced in CDs to enhance the n→π* transitions and efficient intersystem crossing (ISC), which can result in effective RTP emission for CDs [34,35]. Furthermore, the RTP emission can also be improved by embedding CDs into some matrix to limit the vibration/rotation of CDs [36–40]. In the past decades, the research on phosphorescent CDs has made rapid growth owing to their inherent merits such as long luminescence lifetime and avoiding background fluorescence interference, which enables them attractive candidates of promising nanomaterial suited for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications [41–43]. Therefore, some CDs-based room temperature phosphorescent materials caused by water have been developed and used for anti-counterfeiting [44,45]. However, there are few reports about the CDs-based RTP sensors for trace water detection in organic solvents. In addition, test strips (TS) have become an increasingly popular indicator due to their use-friendliness, rapidity, inexpensive nature and specificity [46,47]. Therefore, it is of great significance and practical value to construct RTP-TS based on CDs for visual and rapid water detection with high sensitivity and faster response.

Herein, a new RTP test strip (RTP-TS) based on CDs was successfully prepared and used for visual water detection in organic solvents (Fig. 1 ). N-doped CDs (N-CDs) was firstly synthesized with a hydrothermal treatment of isophthalic acid (IPA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) (Fig. 1a). The obtained N-CDs emit bright blue fluorescence in aqueous solution, and weak RTP emission at solid state (Fig. 1a, I). After embedded N-CDs into polyacrylic acid (PAA) matrix, the RTP of N-CDs/PAA can be greatly enhanced (Fig. 1a, II). RTP-TS was simply constructed by coating N-CDs/PAA on filter paper (Fig. 1b), which can emit bright blue fluorescence (FL) and green phosphorescence (PL) with UV excitation on and off, respectively. More interesting is that the PL of RTP-TS can be quenched evidently by water (Fig. 1b), which makes the constructed RTP-TS can be used for water detection in organic solvents. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of water detection in organic solvents based on long-lived RTP-TS with desired properties, indicating its great potential applications in sensing.

Ethylenediamine (EDA), isophthalic acid (IPA), acetonitrile (ACN), anhydrous calcium chloride (CaCl2) and molecular sieves were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). Polyacrylic acid (PAA) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Absolute ethanol, acetone, tetrahydrofuran (THF), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) and potassium permanganate (KMnO4) were bought from Jinshan Chemical Reagent Company (Chengdu, China). Phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5) and calcium oxide (CaO) were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). The organic solvents were dehydrated before use, the other chemicals used in the experiments are of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs were synthesized with a hydrothermal method using IPA and EDA as precursors. Typically, IPA (4.15 g) was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water, 7 mL EDA was then added into the above aqueous solution under continuous stirring. The obtained colourless mixed solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and then purified in 1000 Da dialysis bag for 12 h. Finally, a light-yellow powder of N-CDs was obtained with a vacuum-drying at 40 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CDs based RTP test strip (RTP-TS)
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical process, 2.0 g PAA was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water with a continuous stirring at 55 °C for 30 min, 0.5 g synthesized N-CDs was then added into the above PAA solution and stirred for another 1 h. The RTP test paper was fabricated by coating the N-CDs/PAA solution on a filter paper with a drip-coating method and then annealed at 80 °C for 30 min. At last, the RTP test paper was cut into strips (2 cm × 1 cm) for further use.

Transmission electron microscopy was obtained using an H-800 electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out using a TD-3500 X-ray powder diffractometer (Tongda, China) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on a Hitachi U-2900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The Nicolet 6700 Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Sugar Land, TX, USA) was used to record the FT-IR spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were acquired on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, America) with Al Kα excitation source (1486.6 eV) running at 15 kV. The XPS were processed by the Casa XPS software. The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were measured by Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Phosphorescence lifetime was obtained on a Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, America) with a Spectra LED (280 nm, S-280) as the excitation source and a picosecond photon detection module (PPD-850) as the detector.

All organic solvents were of analytical grade, and a dehydration process was carried out to remove trace water before the testing. For anhydrous ethanol, 1 g CaO was added into 100 mL ethanol and refluxed for 2 h. The solvent was then distilled and stored in a sealed glass vessel with activated 4 Å molecular sieves contained. For acetone, 100 mL acetone mixed with KMnO4 was refluxed until the purple no longer disappears. After that, the solvent was distilled and stored in sealed glass vessels with anhydrous CaCl2 contained. For anhydrous ACN, 1 g P2O5 was added to 100 mL ACN solvent and refluxed for 12 h. After distillation and separation by filtration, the obtained anhydrous ACN was stored in a sealed glass container for further use. For THF, 100 mL of solvent with 1 g MgSO4 was refluxed for 12 h in a round bottomed flask. The solvent was then distilled and stored in a sealed glass flask.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RTP-TS detection of water in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: Four kinds of organic solvents, including ethanol, acetone, ACN and THF, were selected as the samples to demonstrate the application of RTP-TS for water detection. Firstly, different concentrations of water in four organic solvents (v/v) were prepared, separately. Then, the PL intensity of RTP-TS was measured in each organic solvent without and with water contained. The water content is calculated according to the quenching efficiency (P/P0), where P and P0 indicates the PL intensity of RTP-TS in organic solvents with and without water content, respectively. All experiments were performed at room temperature. The images were taken by a smart phone camera under a UV lamp (365 nm).

It has been reported that presence of heteroatom and the existence of CO and CN bonds are beneficial to increase the n→π* transitions and the efficient intersystem crossing (ISC), which can promote effective RTP emission for CDs [33,34]. Therefore, N doped CDs (N-CDs) were selected to design and construct RTP materials. In this work, N-CDs was synthesized with a simple hydrothermal method, and the reaction of crosslink polymerization, dehydration, carbonization for IPA and EDA can be occurred in this process, which leads to the formation of N-CDs. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shown in Fig. 2 a reveals the N-CDs are of quasi-spherical shape and well dispersed in aqueous solution, and the average size of N-CDs is 2.2 nm with a narrow size distribution of 1.0–3.5 nm. Fig. S1 displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of N-CDs and IPA, respectively. The disappeared diffraction peaks of IPA indicate its carbonization in the synthesized process, and the typical diffraction peak for N-CDs at 22.3° reflects the formation of amorphous N-CDs. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis is used to investigate the surface functional groups present on N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the broad absorption peak between 3500 and 3000 cm−1 should be attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH/-NH [48,49], which leads to the excellent dispensability of N-CDs in aqueous solution. In addition, the disappearance of the characteristic absorption band for IPA between 3000 and 2500 cm−1 indicates de-structure and carbonization of IPA in hydrothermal process. The peak at 1643 cm−1 should be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO for N-CDs, which has a lower wavenumber shift compared to IPA (1688 cm−1). The absorption peak at 1544 cm−1 should be originated from the stretching vibration of CN or bending vibration of N–H [50]. The band at 1303 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–N [32,51]. These results above not only prove the formation of N-CDs from IPA and EDA, but also demonstrate the presence of rich O- and N-contained groups on N-CDs surface, such as –OH, –NH2, –CO, –CN. The surface functional groups are further studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). From Fig. 2c, the characteristic peaks for C 1s (284.6 eV), N 1s (400.78 eV), and O 1s (530.69 eV) indicates the main contained C, N, O elements in N-CDs, the higher content of N (12.7%) and O (17.0%) element indicates the presence of abundant O- and N-contained groups on N-CDs. Fig. 2d shows the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s, four peaks at 284.34 eV, 284.79 eV, 286.20 eV and 287.92 eV can be attributed to CC, C–C, C–N and CN/CO respectively [52,53], demonstrating the existence of CN, CO groups on N-CDs surface. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2e) is deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.28 eV, 400.68 eV, and 401.28 eV, which can be fitted to C–N–C/CN, N-(C)3, and N–H [54], respectively. The O 1s region possesses two peaks at 530.48 eV and 531.38 eV (Fig. 2f), corresponding to the CO and C–O–C/O–H groups [55]. These XPS results also confirm the presence of CN/CO, –OH, –NH2 groups on N-CDs, which agrees well with the results from FT-IR. These heteroatoms contained groups will play an important role in phosphorescence of N-CDs.

Optical property is one of the most important properties of N-CDs, which is therefore investigated in detail. Fig. 3 a displays the UV–vis, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs aqueous solution. From the UV–vis spectrum (black line), two obvious adsorption peaks located at 275 nm and 350 nm can be clearly observed. The new generated adsorption at 350 nm compared with IPA and EDA (Fig. S2) should be attributed to the n→π* transitions of CN/CO from N-CDs [56,57]. Insets of Fig. 3a displays the photograph of N-CDs aqueous solution under the irradiation of daylight (I) and UV light (II), respectively. The bright blue FL emission demonstrates its excellent fluorescent property. The maximum emission located at 462 nm can be assigned to the radiative of singlet excitations of N-CDs with an optimal excitation peak at 380 nm (Fig. 3a). The fluorescence of N-CDs also exhibits the characteristic excitation-dependent features (Fig. S3) as other CDs reported [58]. However, no phosphorescent emission for N-CDs is observed and detected under this condition, which may be due to the phosphorescence quenching caused by dissolved oxygen and water in solution [31,59]. To investigate these effects, nitrogen is bubbled into the solution to remove the dissolved oxygen, and no phosphorescence is detected either (Fig. S4), which indicates the dissolved oxygen is not the key point for phosphorescence quenching in this system. The effect of water on PL of N-CDs was also studied by measuring the optical property of solid N-CDs powder. It can be seen that the solid N-CDs powder not only exhibits bright blue fluorescence emission (inset of Fig. 3b, I) under the irradiation of UV light (365 nm), but also emit weak green phosphorescence after turning off the UV light (inset of Fig. 3b, II), demonstrating the RTP of N-CDs is mainly hampered by water, which has been proved to be one of the most effective triple state quenching agents by nonradiative decay processes [16]. Fig. 3b shows the phosphorescence excitation and emission spectra of solid N-CDs. From the results we can see that the N-CDs powder has a weak PL emission at 527 nm with the excitation at 360 nm. However, this weak PL intensity of N-CDs is not conducive to its application in sensing with high visual sensitivity. To overcome this problem, polyacrylic acid (PAA) was introduced as matrix to enhance the phosphorescent intensity of N-CDs by suppressing its nonradiative decay process, which has been demonstrated to contribute to PL emission at room temperature [60]. Fig. 3c shows the phosphorescence/fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of solid N-CDs/PAA. We can see that the phosphorescence intensity of N-CDs/PAA is greatly enhanced compared to N-CDs (Fig. 3b). The bright green phosphorescence emission from N-CDs/PAA can also be clearly observed (Inset of Fig. 3c). However, N-CDs/PAA exhibits a blue-shift phosphorescence emission (510 nm) compared to N-CDs (527 nm), which should be attributed to the introduction of PAA changes the excitation state of N-CDs. Fig. 3d displays the time-resolved photoluminescence decay curves of N-CDs/PAA and N-CDs. The spectra have to be fitted with a di-exponential function with lifetime components to be 0.239 s and 1.37 s of N-CDs/PAA, the lifetimes, total contributions and complete fitting parameters are summarized in Table S1. The average phosphorescence lifetime for N-CDs/PAA is measured to be 0.769 s, which is longer than that of N-CDs (0.309 s) [31], further demonstrating the better PL property of N-CDs/PAA than N-CDs. Fig. 3e shows the photographs of N-CDs/PAA powder with the excitation light (365 nm) on and off. It can be seen that the N-CDs/PAA powder can emit strong fluorescent emission under the UV light irradiation, and emit bright green phosphorescence, which can be clearly observed by naked eyes in 10 s after turning off the irradiation source. The corresponding luminescence video of the N-CDs/PAA powder is shown in Video S1. These results not only indicate the excellent phosphorescence properties of N-CDs/PAA at room temperature, but also demonstrate the potential application of N-CDs/PAA for construction of visual sensors based on RTP.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109226

The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 22

ce:section.ce:section-title: RTP mechanism for N-CDs and N-CDs/PAA
ce:section.ce:para:
The FT-IR and XPS characterizations illustrate the synthesized N-CDs is rich in –CO, –CN, –OH/-NH2 groups, which has previously been demonstrated to favour the production of triplet excitons, facilitate the efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) and generate RTP [38]. Therefore, the origin of RTP for N-CDs was investigated according to the UV–vis absorption and excitation spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3a and b). It can be seen that the broad RTP excitation band between 320 and 430 nm overlaps with the absorption band of 300–400 nm, indicating the RTP should derive from the CN/CO groups, which has been proved to increase the population of triplet excitons through n→π* transitions and lead to the generation of RTP [61]. In addition, the energy gap (ΔE
ST ) between lowest singlet (S
1 ) and triplet (T
1 ) excitations was quantitatively evaluated based on the results of FL and PL spectra of N-CDs. The results indicate that there is a 43 nm Stokes’ shifts between the fluorescence (484 nm, Fig. S5) and the phosphorescence (527 nm, Fig. 3b) emission for N-CDs, which is consistent with the energy gap of 0.21 eV (Fig. 4 a). This small ΔE
ST makes the spin-orbit coupling efficient and further facilitates the RTP generation of N-CDs, although the phosphorescence is relatively weak. By embedding N-CDs into PAA matrix, the phosphorescent intensity of N-CDs/PAA is largely improved due to the formation of H-bonds between PAA chains and N-CDs (Fig. 4b), which can be demonstrated from the FT-IR spectra changes of PAA, N-CDs, and N-CDs/PAA (Fig. S6). After introducing of PAA, the adsorption peak of N-CDs/PAA between 3500 and 3000 cm−1 (-OH/-NH) is significantly decreased (Fig. S6a), and the peak at 1643 cm−1 of N-CDs shifts to 1615 cm−1 (Fig. S6b), these changes should be ascribed to the formation of hydrogen bonds (H-bonds) between PAA and N-CDs [62]. The formation of H-bonds can increase the rigidity as well as restrict the vibration of N-CDs, which effectively minimize the vibrational/rotation dissipation between PAA and N-CDs and then enhance the ISC process from S
1 to T
1 as much as possible [63–65]. Therefore, the introduction of PAA greatly contributes to the enhanced RTP of N-CDs, and the longer RTP lifetime and higher RTP intensity of N-CDs/PAA than N-CDs should be ascribed to the Crosslink Enhanced Emission Effect (CEE) [66].

Based on this, a possible mechanism of RTP for N-CDs/PAA can be depicted as shown in Fig. 4. i) The presence of N and O elements increase the single and triplet excitation levels of N-CDs, which can decreases the energy gap between S
1 and T
1 , and allows the efficient spin-orbit coupling and ISC, through which some of the excited electrons can transfer from S
1 to T
1 , and strong phosphorescence emission can be obtained due to the radiation recombination transitions from T
1 to S
0 [67], as illustrated in Fig. 4a ii) The presence of –CO, –OH and –NH2 groups on N-CDs surface induce the formation of H-bonds with PAA matrix (Fig. 4b), which can not only facilitate the stability of the triplet excited states, but also restrict the varication/rotation of N-CDs, enabling efficient room-temperature phosphorescence at last [68]. More interesting is that this bright green RTP emission can be effectively quenched by the presence of H2O in N-CDs/PAA system due to the breaking of original H-bonds between N-CDs and PAA by H2O (Fig. 4b), which results in the weakening of system rigidity and the strengthening the varication/rotation of –CO, –CN and other groups on N-CDs. These groups changes can also be demonstrated from the FI-IR spectra of N-CDs/PAA with and without water contained (Fig. S7). Further, the phosphorescent lifetime of N-CDs/PAA was also measured in ethanol solutions with different water contents (0, 8%, 16%, 24%, 32%, v/v) at room temperature (Fig. S8a and Table S1). It can be seen that the phosphorescence lifetime of N-CDs/PAA has a slight decrease with the increase of water content (Fig. S8b). This may be the breaking of H-bonds between N-CDs and PAA by H2O can significantly reduce the stability of N-CDs excited triplet state and ultimately increase its probability of non-radioactive transition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the experimental phenomena that the RTP of solid N-CDs/PAA can be quenched efficiently by water, but the fluorescence intensity was not affected (Fig. S9). Therefore, we speculate that this N-CDs/PAA composite can be used to construct RTP sensor for water detection in organic solvents. To facilitate and broad its applications, RTP test strip (RTP-TS) was fabricated by coating N-CDs/PAA on a common filter paper (Fig. 1b). The testing was carried out at room temperature, and four organic solvents of anhydrous ethanol, acetone, ACN and THF was selected. The sensing ability of RTP-TS to H2O was evaluated by measuring its RTP intensity changes in each organic solvent with and without water contained. First, the interaction time between N-CDs/PAA and water in ethanol was measured as shown in Fig. S10. When the RTP-TS was immersed in an ethanol solution contained 26.0% water (v/v), its phosphorescence intensity decreases gradually and then keep stable at about 15 min, suggesting the reasonable response of RTP-TS for water detection. Fig. 5 a shows the observable PL changes of RTP-TS in ethanol solvent with water content from 0 to 20%. The obvious reorganization by naked eyes indicates that RTP-TS can be used for semi-quantitative water detection more conveniently and quickly. Fig. 5b shows the phosphorescent spectra of RTP-TS in anhydrous ethanol with different H2O contained. The gradually decreased PL intensity as the water content increase from 1.0% to 30.0% (v/v) proves the sensitive response of RTP-TS to H2O in ethanol. A reasonable linear correlation (R2 = 0.9932) between the quenching efficiency (P/P0) and the water content in the range 1.0 %–24.0% (v/v) can be obtained as shown in Fig. 5c, and the linear regression equation is expressed as P/P0 = 1.073–0.0435C, where C is the content of water in ethanol (v/v), P and P0 indicates the phosphorescent intensity of RTP-TS (510 nm) in ethanol with and without water, respectively. Fig. 5d–f shows the response of RTP-TS to water in acetone solvent. Comparing with ethanol, the RTP-TS also exhibits an obviously visual response to water in acetone solvent (Fig. 5d). However, a red shift of RTP emission (515 nm) in acetone is observed compared to that in ethanol (510 nm), which should be due to the effects of solvent. The RTP intensity also decreases sensitively with the increasing of H2O (Fig. 5e), and the linear regression equation can be expressed as P/P0 = 1.035–0.1237C with a reasonable correlation of 0.9998 between 0.2%~8.0% (v/v) (Fig. 5f). Furthermore, the visual and quantitative detection of water in ACN and THF based on RTP-TS were also carried out with the similar procedure, and the results are shown in Fig. S11. The excellent response of RTP-TS to water in the range of 0.2 %–6.0% for ACN (P/P0 = 1.024–0.1497C, R2 = 0.9984), and 0.5 %–4.0% for THF (P/P0 = 1.138–0.2768C, R2 = 0.9939) further indicates the universal applications of this general RTP sensor for water detection in organic solvents. The limit of detection (LOD) for water of RTP-TS were calculated as 0.071% in ethanol, 0.027% in acetone, 0.021% in ACN，and 0.011% in THF based on 3σ/slope. As shown in Table S2, the LODs of this work is lower than that of most fluorescence sensors based on carbon material, and exhibits comparable or better performance compared to other Ir or Cu materials. Therefore, the advantages of low-cost, simple preparation and environment-friendly make this RTP-TS have a potential application for water detection in organic solvents.

The stability and reproducibility of constructed RTP-TS for water detection has also been investigated to demonstrate its potential applications in organic solvents. Fig. 6 a shows the phosphorescence intensity changes of RTP-TS in four organic solvents with continuous UV irradiation for 1 h. The unchanged RTP intensity demonstrates the excellent stability of RTP-TS in samples. Fig. 6b displays the response of 10 pieces of RTP-TS to water in ethanol solvent without (red) and with (black) H2O contained, and the smaller RSD of 3.15% proves its good reproducibility for practical application. In addition, to further demonstrate the accuracy of RTP-TS in routine analysis, the recovery of water detection in organic solvent was determined by testing two different water contents in each sample and comparing the results. As shown in Table 1 , the satisfactory recoveries (98.5–110%) and relative standard deviation (1.02%–4.99%) demonstrates the excellent accuracy and reliability of RTP-TS for water detection in these organic solvents. In a word, this constructed RTP-TS based on N-CDs/PAA will have wide applications for water detection in organic solvents due to the advantages of easy preparation, inexpensive, environmentally friendly, excellent stability, and non-background interference.

In summary, we report a facile strategy to construct test strip with excellent RTP properties and stability based on N-doped CDs. The N-CDs synthesized with a simple hydrothermal method has been demonstrated with abundant rich O- and N-contained groups (such as –CO, –CN, –OH, –NH2), which can promote the n→π* transitions, increase the probability of ISC and therefore enhance the triplet excitons responsible for RTP. By embedding N-CDs in PAA matrix, the obtained N-CDs/PAA can exhibit bright green RTP emission with an average lifetime of 0.769 s under 350 nm excitation, which should be attributed to the introduction of PAA can stabilize the triplet excitons and suppress the nonradiative quenching process of N-CDs by the formed H-bonds between N-CDs and PAA chains. More interesting is that the presence of H2O can destroy the H-bonds and thus quench the RTP of N-CDs/PAA. Based on this, N-CDs/PAA was used to construct RTP-TS for visual and quantitative detection of water in several organic solvents. Testing results demonstrate the sensitive response of RTP-TS to water with excellent stability, accuracy and reliability. In a word, this is a green and simple method for construction visual RTP-TS with excellent phosphorescent properties, it will open a new way to develop an easy, sensitive, and economical visual detection device for water analysis in organic solvents.
Bing Wang: Design and carry out the experiments, synthesis of materials, analysis the data, write and edit manuscript.
Xiaoying Yuan: Synthesis and characterize materials, analysis the data.
Xu Lv: Testing and analysis the data.
Yongjun Mei: Analysis the data, reviewing and editing the manuscript.
Huaqiao Peng: Writing and reviewing the original manuscript.
Lin Li: Characterizations and analysis the data.
Yong Guo: Reviewing and editing the manuscript.
Juan Du: Writing-reviewing and editing the manuscript.
Baozhan Zheng: Supervision, analysis the data, writing-reviewing and editing the manuscript.
Dan Xiao: Writing and editing the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is ﬁnancially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1833124, 21876117 and U1833202) and Sichuan Science and Technology Program (No.2019YFG0218). Prof. Peng Wu in Analytical & Testing Centre at Sichuan University was also appreciated for the optical measurements and analysis of materials.
